data.title: Solid-State Batteries: The Next Revolution In Electric Vehicle Battery Technology?
data.description:
What are solid-state batteries and why the excitement around them?

In solid-state batteries (SSBs) the flammable liquid electrolyte found in conventional lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is replaced with a solid alternative. This shift enables components to be packed more tightly, eliminates leakage risks and allows for the use of lithium metal anodes.

These anodes can achieve higher theoretical energy capacities compared to graphite-based systems used in LIBs. Solid electrolytes also offer greater tolerance to high voltages and temperatures. The result is a new generation of batteries that are safer, charge faster, last longer and deliver more range than today’s best electric vehicle (EV) batteries.

What is driving the push for better batteries?

The global transition to net zero demands a revolution in energy storage technology. To meet 2050 climate targets, the International Energy Agency estimates annual EV battery demand will grow from 1TWh in 2024 to more than 3TWh in 2030.

With the EU planning to ban petrol vehicle sales by 2035, battery innovation is no longer optional. Conventional LIB technology is approaching its practical limits, meaning that further improvements in energy density, efficiency and cost are increasingly difficult to achieve without making fundamental changes. Therefore, today’s batteries cannot deliver the performance leap required to make EVs as convenient and affordable as combustion-engine cars.

Why have we not seen SSBs in vehicles yet?

Despite their potential, SSBs face major technical and manufacturing hurdles. Lithium metal anodes expand and contract during charging, creating mechanical stress that degrades performance over time. Microscopic dendrites can form, penetrating the solid electrolyte and causing short circuits.

Producing solid electrolytes that conduct ions as efficiently as liquids, while remaining stable and durable, remains a central challenge. Furthermore, manufacturing high-quality solid components requires precision engineering and stringent control, driving up costs. Bridging the gap between laboratory success and mass production at gigafactory scale remains the critical hurdle.

Which companies are leading the race?

Toyota (TM) holds over 8,400 patents related to SSBs and has partnered with Idemitsu Kosan (IDKOY) and Sumitomo Metal Mining (SMMYY) for mass production of SSBs. QuantumScape (QS), which is partnered with Volkswagen (VWAGY), has demonstrated prototype cells achieving 844Wh/L energy density and 12-minute fast charging. Samsung SDI (SSNLF) has joined forces with Solid Power (SLDP) and BMW Group (BAMXF) to produce prototype SSBs with energy densities of 900Wh/L.

What about Tesla?

Tesla (TSLA) has made no official commitment to SSBs and may be betting on alternative approaches to achieve similar performance gains or positioning itself to acquire solid-state capability once the technology matures.

How is the global battery supply chain shifting?

East Asia still dominates global battery innovation and manufacturing. However, North America and Europe are investing heavily in localised production to reduce reliance on Asian supply chains, while emerging markets contribute fresh approaches to materials and design. This diversification is reshaping the industry’s dynamics and driving the need for adaptable, cost-efficient manufacturing.

How can investors play the theme?

Pure-play options include QuantumScape (c $11bn market cap) and Solid Power (c $1bn market cap). QuantumScape offers higher risk/reward with its proprietary ceramic separator, while Solid Power provides a more modest entry point.

Neither is profitable, with meaningful revenues years away. Established automakers like Toyota (c $54.6tn market cap), BMW (c €58bn) and Volkswagen (c €52bn) offer indirect exposure with lower volatility.

When will SSBs go mainstream?

Pilot production of SSBs is underway, with prototypes shipping to automakers for testing. QuantumScape and Volkswagen plan for field trials to take place in 2026 with mass production targeted for 2028, while Toyota aims to launch vehicles with SSBs by 2027.

Meaningful EV adoption is expected in the early 2030s (according to IDTechEx). In the meantime, semi-SSBs, which combine solid and liquid electrolytes, are reaching the market first, with MG Motor already launching a semi-solid-state EV in China.

Edison insight

Solid-state batteries are widely seen as the next major evolution in EV battery technology, offering a potential step change in energy density and safety. With a projected $10bn market by 2036 (a 53.9% CAGR), according to IDTechEx, and major automakers racing to commercialise, the technology represents a compelling long-term theme.

However, with average lithium-ion cell costs around $80/kWh and solid-state batteries not expected to reach comparable cost levels until the late 2020s, mass adoption is likely to remain a late-decade story. Investors should focus on companies with proven manufacturing pathways and strong automotive partnerships rather than laboratory breakthroughs alone.

Original Post

data.title: Banco Bradesco Is No Longer Broken, But It's Too Early To Get Aggressive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As usual, I like to be very clear about my stance right from the start, and I’m currently at HOLD on Banco Bradesco (BBD).

The bank is clearly moving in the right direction, but I still think it’s too early to get aggressive with the stock.

The 3Q25 results show a real operational improvement. Recurring net income came in at R$ 6.2 billion, and ROAE reached 14.7%, extending the gradual recovery trend the bank has been delivering over the past few quarters.

On the risk side (which is where I tend to be extra demanding when it comes to banks), the signals remain far healthier than in the recent past. Non-performing loans above 90 days were stable at 4.1% in September, and the cost of credit edged up only marginally to 3.3%, largely explained by specific reinforcements in wholesale banking and the Banco John Deere consolidation. On top of that, the restructured loan portfolio continues to shrink, which is exactly the kind of balance-sheet “cleanup” that improves earnings quality over time.

There’s also one area I particularly like here, which is Insurance. This business remains a very strong pillar for the group, posting R$ 5.7 billion in quarterly results and a 22.4% ROAE. That significantly reduces the bank’s reliance on the traditional banking operation alone to support the investment case.

That said, my main reason for not upgrading this to a BUY is that profitability is still not high enough for me to confidently bet on a sustained re-rating. With large banks, I like to see ROAE improving not just because of the cycle or tactical optimizations, but through a combination of: (1) a better credit mix, (2) a genuinely tamed cost of risk, and (3) operating expenses running on a more predictable track.

What keeps my enthusiasm in check is the fact that Bradesco is still investing heavily, and expenses remain pressured on a year-over-year basis — even though there are signs of control once you adjust for specific effects. In practice, the bank is still in the "planting" phase, and I want to see a few more quarters of that effort translating into higher ROAE.

The good news is that Bradesco has enough capital to keep running without stretching risk. Tier 1 capital stands at 13.4%, core capital at 11.4%, and efficiency is hovering around 50%, according to management.

So, to sum it up, Bradesco is clearly turning the page, but I’m not ready to buy into the idea that it has already become “a new Itaú (ITUB)”. Once ROAE proves it can move to a higher level with more consistency and without relying on one-off items, then I think it will make sense to get more aggressive.

A Giant Bank With A Long Memory

Banco Bradesco is one of the largest private banks in Latin America and a central pillar of Brazil’s financial system. Founded in 1943, the bank built a broad nationwide presence, serving tens of millions of clients across retail banking, corporate banking, wealth management, and insurance.

In terms of scale, Bradesco is a heavyweight. The institution reports annual revenues above R$ 250 billion, a loan portfolio approaching R$ 900 billion, and a meaningful presence across virtually every major segment of the Brazilian banking system.

Beyond traditional banking, the group controls Bradesco Seguros, the clear leader in Brazil’s insurance market, which adds an important and recurring source of earnings to the overall franchise.

Historically, Bradesco has been viewed as a more defensive asset within the Brazilian equities, largely due to its strong cash-generation capacity and its consistent track record of shareholder distributions.

When it comes to dividends, the bank maintains a steady payout policy, with frequent distributions throughout the year. At current price levels (R$ 20.79), the dividend yield stands around 6%–7% annually, making the stock relevant even for income-focused investors, despite the bank still being in a recovery phase.

The Numbers Are Cleaner, But Still A Work In Progress

If I had to sum up Bradesco’s 3Q25 in a single sentence, I’d say it was a quarter of operational growth with better quality, but still without that clear “spark” that completely changes how the market prices the bank.

Revenue and Earnings Composition

The starting point is that the bank has returned to a much more consistent pace of revenue growth.

Total revenues reached R$ 35.0 billion in 3Q25, up 3.0% quarter over quarter and 13.1% year over year, driven simultaneously by net interest income, fees, and insurance. That matters because it’s far healthier than relying on a single line to carry results.

Total net interest income came in at R$ 18.7 billion, up 3.7% q/q and 16.9% y/y. Within that, what stands out to me is the traction coming from the bank’s core business, which is its relationship with clients.

Net interest income with clients reached R$ 18.6 billion, growing 4.8% q/q and 19.0% y/y. And there’s an important detail here that helps explain why this line is improving.

Average spreads increased from 8.8% in 2Q25 to 9.0% in 3Q25, which management largely attributes to better funding efficiency and an improved credit mix.

Meanwhile, market-related NII was very small, at R$ 99 million, and declined sequentially, something the bank itself had already anticipated for the quarter.

In other words, I don’t see results being “carried” by a treasury win. They are anchored in what Bradesco can generate through its customer base, pricing discipline, and funding structure.

Loan Growth and Mix

Another relevant point is that the loan book also continued to grow, expanding 9.6% year over year and 1.6% quarter over quarter as of September 2025, with strength in SMEs and retail.

And this brings up an aspect that, for banks, is extremely valuable. The mix is becoming slightly more defensive. The share of collateralized loans increased from 58.5% in June 2025 to 59.5% in September 2025.

That doesn’t solve everything, but in my view, it does help reduce the volatility of credit costs across the cycle.

Asset Quality and Delinquency

Looking at the most closely watched metric — non-performing loans over 90 days — the bank showed stability, with the ratio holding at 4.1% in September 2025.

The report highlights that, over 12 months, this figure declined by 0.1 percentage point, marking the second consecutive quarter of stability at the consolidated level.

What I find more informative, though, is the breakdown by segment. In SMEs, asset quality improved meaningfully, with NPLs down 0.6 p.p. q/q and 1.5 p.p. compared to September 2024.

In retail, management acknowledges that delinquency was affected by the consolidation of Banco John Deere. According to the report, without this effect, the overall NPL ratio would have declined by 0.1 p.p. in the quarter.

There’s another health signal that has also improved and that many investors overlook, which is risk stages.

The bank shows progress in portfolio quality, with 4% of stage 3 loans curing during the period and 12% of stage 2 exposures migrating to stage 1. Over 12 months, the combined share of stages 1 and 2 increased by 1.2 p.p., reaching 92.3% as of September 2025.

Finally, the data point that best supports the “house-cleaning” narrative is the restructured loan book.

It declined 22% compared to September 2024, with a 1.6 p.p. reduction in its share of the expanded loan portfolio, and the bank reports provisioning at roughly 2x loans overdue by more than 90 days.

In my view, this is the detail that truly validates the idea that the worst is already behind Bradesco.

Cost of Credit and Provisions

Even with delinquency stable, the cost of credit edged up slightly.

The bank reported a cost of credit of 3.3% in 3Q25, up from 3.2% in 2Q25, mainly due to additional provisioning in specific wholesale exposures and again the impact from Banco John Deere.

On the recurring P&L statement, expanded loan loss provisions totaled R$ 8.56 billion in the quarter, and the LLP-to-loan ratio shows a mild upward trend throughout 2025, reaching 3.3% on an annualized basis in 3Q25.

My takeaway here is that the bank is not “loosening” credit standards to chase growth. It is doing the opposite, acting in a conscious and disciplined way, reinforcing buffers where necessary.

That’s a positive from a risk standpoint, because it’s conservative. But conservative, at times, also means ROAE improving more slowly.

Expenses, Efficiency, and Footprint Adjustment

This is where the discussion becomes more sensitive, in my view, because Bradesco is investing heavily and reorganizing its operations. And as everyone knows, that comes at a cost, even when management emphasizes discipline.

In 3Q25, personnel and administrative expenses reached R$ 12.9 billion, up 5.5% year over year and 3.3% quarter over quarter.

According to the presentation, total operating expenses were R$ 16.5 billion, and the quarterly efficiency ratio stood at 50.1%, with a slight sequential deterioration of 0.2 percentage point. What complicates the reading is that part of this pressure comes from items that make sense, given the bank’s current phase.

The report points to higher spending on data processing, communication, and marketing, tied to investments in technology and the customer journey. There is also the personnel dynamic, which includes profit-sharing payments and wage adjustments.

The bank reports a reduction of 490 employees in the quarter and 2,361 over the past 12 months, in line with its strategy to optimize the cost to serve, while simultaneously strengthening technology and operations teams.

There is also the physical footprint adjustment, which is far from cosmetic. In the presentation, Bradesco shows a sharp reduction in service points, down 1,603 compared to September 2024.

All of this is extremely important for my HOLD thesis. Efficiency is what separates a “bank in recovery” from a bank that returns to premium status — and in my view, Bradesco is still in the middle of that journey.

Insurance as a Pillar of Profitability

This is probably Bradesco’s strongest card — and one that not everyone fully appreciates. The insurance group posted net income of R$ 2.5 billion in 3Q25, with a 22.4% ROAE.

In the report, the bank details that results from insurance, pension, and capitalization operations totaled R$ 5.7 billion in 3Q25, with the improvement driven by lower claims ratios and better financial results. Industrial results accounted for roughly two-thirds of the total in the period.

When you put this alongside the core banking operation, it becomes clear why Bradesco has been able to weather a longer transformation process.

In short, the bank has a parallel profitability engine, which, in my view, reduces the risk of the turnaround turning into an endless story.

Another important point is that the bank is well capitalized for this kind of process. Its Tier 1 Basel ratio stood at 13.4% in 3Q25, improving both sequentially and year over year.

That gives Bradesco room to adjust its credit mix, invest in transformation, and still maintain comfortable capital buffers.

What is Structural and What Still Depends on the Cycle

In my opinion, revenue growth and the shift toward a more collateralized credit mix look structural, because they depend primarily on internal decisions and commercial execution.

The cleanup of the restructured portfolio and the improvement across risk stages also point to a better-built credit quality, not just a temporary relief.

On the other hand, a 3.3% cost of credit and current delinquency levels still carry some sensitivity to the economic cycle and to specific effects, such as the consolidation of Banco John Deere and targeted reinforcements in wholesale banking.

Efficiency, despite early signs of adjustment, remains the area most dependent on continuity. The bank is investing, cutting physical infrastructure, and reshaping its workforce, and that typically takes time to translate into a more visible margin improvement at the ROAE level.

So if I had to sum up Bradesco’s financials in a concise way, I’d say the numbers are clearly better and cleaner than they used to be, and the bank has already stopped "bleeding".

But for the market to pay a higher price for Bradesco, it will need to see this improvement repeated over several more quarters, with efficiency moving in the right direction and no surprises on the cost of credit.

This Is Not A Mispriced Stock

When I look at Banco Bradesco’s share price and valuation multiples, what I see is a cautious (almost conservative) pricing, waiting for stronger proof before paying up for the bank.

Today, BBD trades around 1.1x–1.2x book value and roughly 9x–10x forward earnings, depending on the estimates you use. For a bank of this size, with a diversified operation and a strong insurance arm, it can look very cheap at first glance.

But once you put Bradesco side by side with its peers, the discount starts to make sense. Itaú trades at much higher multiples (around 2.2x P/B) because it consistently delivers ROE above 17%–20%, with far superior efficiency and much lower earnings volatility. Banco do Brasil (BDORY), on the other hand, trades even cheaper than Bradesco, but carries its own set of risks related to agribusiness credit defaults and political interference. Bradesco sits right in between those two extremes.

In other words, the market is not saying Bradesco is a bad bank, only that it doesn’t deserve a premium yet.

This discount also becomes clear when you look at Bradesco’s own history. In past cycles, when the bank was delivering profitability closer to sector leaders, trading above 1.5x book value was not unusual. Today, the market clearly does not believe that level is sustainable in the near term, and, to be fair, the numbers don’t force anyone to believe it yet.

In my view, the current valuation works as a kind of safety net. It significantly reduces the risk of major disappointments, because a good portion of the skepticism is already embedded in the price. This is not a bank trading as if everything were perfect, but rather as if the recovery will be slow, uneven, and still prone to setbacks.

The issue is that, at the same time, this valuation does not create an automatic upside trigger.

For the stock to move out of this range and trade closer to higher-quality private-sector peers, Bradesco needs to deliver something very specific. This would be a clear and sustained improvement in profitability, with ROAE moving to a more competitive level and operating efficiency finally converging. Without that, the discount is likely to persist, even if results remain broadly solid.

That’s why I don’t buy into the narrative that Bradesco is “too cheap.” To me, it is cheap for the bank it is today, not for the bank it used to be, and certainly not for the bank some investors hope it will become again.

At the end of the day, the current valuation does exactly what it should in a case like this. It limits downside risk, offers reasonable dividend income, and buys some time, instead of promising a quick re-rating. And that is precisely why, at this stage, the price supports a HOLD, not an aggressive BUY.

The Real Risk Is Mediocrity

When I think about risk at Banco Bradesco, I’m not talking about the obvious ones like solvency or going back to a stress scenario. In my view, that kind of major risk is already far behind it. What’s left now is a set of more subtle risks, but ones that make all the difference for valuation and investor returns.

The first is that Bradesco is in the middle of a meaningful operational transformation, cutting physical infrastructure, investing heavily in technology, and trying to improve efficiency all at once, and that is almost never a linear process. There’s a real risk the bank does “almost everything right,” but delivers efficiency gains more slowly than the market expects... and with large banks, every delayed quarter matters. If efficiency remains stuck around the 50% level for too long, ROE tends to improve only modestly, even if revenues keep moving up.

The second risk lies in credit, even with current indicators under control. Delinquency has stabilized, the restructured portfolio has shrunk, and the credit mix has become somewhat more defensive, which is great. Still, the bank remains exposed to Brazil’s domestic cycle, which is naturally volatile (trust me on that). A sharper economic slowdown, a specific shock in consumer credit, or even new isolated cases in wholesale banking could delay the normalization of credit costs. It’s not as if that would destroy my thesis, but it would limit the speed of the profitability recovery.

There is also a competitiveness risk, which is less visible in quarterly numbers but weighs on the medium term. Bradesco operates in a much more competitive market than the one that helped it build scale in the past. Itaú continues to deliver superior efficiency, digital banks keep pressuring margins in certain products, and the cost of staying technologically relevant is permanent — and now heavier than ever. If Bradesco fails to turn technology investment into real productivity gains, the bank has the risk of improving… but still lagging behind its peers.

And then comes what, for me, is the most important risk of all, and one that is directly tied to the current valuation, which is the risk of Bradesco improving without surprising anyone.

The numbers can carry on advancing, profits can grow, the balance sheet can look cleaner, and still the market may look at the bank and conclude that this was more or less expected. In that scenario, the stock keeps paying dividends, remains stable, but the multiple doesn’t move.

In short, this is not a risk of meaningful capital loss, but rather a risk of mediocre returns.

That’s why, when I think about risk today, I don't think about Bradesco getting worse — I think about the bank doing everything right, but not enough to justify paying a higher price for it.

For me, 4Q25 Is About Staying on Track

Bradesco is set to report its 4Q25 results on February 5, and this quarter, to me, is much more about checking consistency than expecting any kind of abrupt turnaround. I see this result far more as a confirmation that the bank is on the right track than as a real “wow” moment.

And this isn’t just my impression. On the 3Q25 call, management was quite clear about the pace the bank intends to maintain:

“Our focus remains on disciplined growth, with strict credit underwriting and a gradual improvement in efficiency. We are not pursuing short-term acceleration at the expense of asset quality or capital discipline.”

Starting from what was already delivered in 3Q25, the baseline is fairly well established. The bank reported R$ 6.2 billion in recurring net income, with a 14.7% ROAE, total revenue of R$ 35.0 billion (+3.0% q/q), and a 3.3% cost of credit, already reflecting specific reinforcements in wholesale credit and the impact from the consolidation of Banco John Deere.

Looking ahead to 4Q, I think the scenario becomes relatively well defined if the bank simply repeats its recent pattern. Assuming revenue growth similar to 3Q (something between 2% and 4% q/q), total revenue should land in the R$ 35.7 to R$ 36.5 billion range. Honestly, I don’t see anything in the disclosed materials or in management’s commentary that points to acceleration beyond that.

On the credit side, the loan book grew 1.6% q/q in 3Q25, and I see management as quite comfortable with that pace. The focus remains on mix, collateral, and risk-adjusted returns, not volume for volume’s sake. Another excerpt from the call helps reinforce this view:

“We continue to prioritize portfolio quality and risk-adjusted returns. The improvement in credit indicators reflects better underwriting and portfolio management, and we do not see the need to loosen criteria to drive growth.”

With the restructured portfolio down 22% y/y and provisions maintaining coverage close to 2x loans past due over 90 days, I believe the cost of credit in 4Q should remain stable, likely between 3.2% and 3.3%. In my view, this significantly reduces the risk of negative surprises, but it also makes it clear that we shouldn’t expect a quick relief coming from this line.

On expenses, I remain a bit more cautious. The fourth quarter usually carries important seasonal effects, such as profit-sharing payments. In 3Q25, the efficiency ratio stood at 50.1%, and the bank itself acknowledges that investments in technology and ongoing operational reorganization are still pressuring costs in the short term. In practice, I see 4Q efficiency still hovering around 49.5% to 50.5%, without a clear structural improvement just yet.

The Insurance operation, in turn, continues to provide meaningful support to consolidated results. In 3Q25, the segment delivered R$ 2.5 billion in profit, with a 22.4% ROAE, and there was no indication of any material change in this dynamic heading into year-end.

Putting all of this together, in my reading, the most plausible scenario for 4Q25 points to recurring net income in the R$ 6.3 to R$ 6.7 billion range, which would imply a ROAE of roughly 14.8% to 15.6%. And here, honestly, the final number should depend much more on how expenses behave than on any positive surprise in revenue or credit.

A Stock To Watch But Not To Chase

At the end of the day, Bradesco today is a very different bank from the one that worried the market a few years ago. Risk has come down, the numbers are more organized, and the operational trajectory makes sense. You can now look at the bank without that constant feeling that something might spiral out of control at any moment.

At the same time, it still lacks the ingredient that truly changes the game, which is profitability at a higher level, showing up in a predictable and sustained way. Until that happens, the market is right to keep some distance and demand a discount.

That’s why I maintain a HOLD position for now.

And apparently, I'm not alone in this, since Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating also has had this same stance since May 2025.

So, for those already invested, the stock makes sense as a patient normalization play, with dividends helping to carry the thesis. For those not yet positioned, however, I don’t see any urgency to jump in. It’s reasonable to watch a few more quarters, track the evolution of efficiency and profitability, and only then decide whether it makes sense to get more aggressive.

data.title: U.S. Dollar Stakes Get Raised - What To Do Now In The Rates Space?
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

The US dollar popped weaker on most crosses late Tuesday. This stemmed from off-the-cuff comments from President Trump along the lines that he's okay with some dollar softness, as it means that other currencies are less likely to devalue versus the dollar, in turn preventing their products from becoming more competitively priced in the US.

This harps back to the so-called Mar-a-Largo accord that voiced frustration that dollar reserve status, while positive, resulted in a dollar that was too strong, in turn making US manufacturing less competitive. Our views on that are here (Q&A).

Remember, it's the US Treasury that has responsibility for the US dollar, not the Federal Reserve. Scott Bessent, on CNBC on Wednesday morning, cited the "strong dollar policy" phrase. So, he has decided not to push in the same direction as President Trump.

Indeed, the dollar does not need a push. It's already moving along the path of least resistance - weaker. It's the unwritten preference of the administration, but not too weak too fast, and not getting to uncomfortable levels. Our FX strategists have also maintained a weaker dollar view.

So what does all this mean for the rates versus FX arbitrage? In recent weeks, we've opined on this for Japan and South Korea and China. Now we focus on the eurozone, or more specifically, Ester verus SOFR.

The FX exposure piece - anticipating a weaker dollar

A weakening in the US dollar versus the euro should not be a huge surprise. It's discounted in the FX forward profile, which is a reflection of the rate differentials between higher US rates and lower eurozone rates. If those spreads represent an equilibrium, then the EUR must appreciate versus the USD. That said, forwards are not always realised.

A great example of that is the recent weakness of the Japanese yen. There, the forwards have not been realised as the rate spreads have not represented an equilibrium. Hence, the rapid tightening in spreads, mostly from higher Japanese market rates.

The big question from an Ester versus SOFR perspective is how the EUR/USD FX rate actually evolves versus the forwards. Our FX strategists have generally viewed the 1.20 to 1.25 range as a path we can absolutely venture along. Beyond that, it gets more hazy.

Anything that threatened 1.30 would look a tad outlandish. So conservatively, we have 1.25 as a critical level, acting as something of a floor. As we glean along the FX forward profile (chart below), we hit 1.24 on the 3yr tenor and we're at 1.26 on a 4yr tenor. That's the area of inflection from a rates tenor perspective.

The EUR/USD exchange rate - shooting to 1.20 area (USD down!)

And for FX forward profile - discounting 1.36, eventually

The spreads piece - steady to tighter anticipated

The bias from the previous section is to avoid being short the euro versus the dollar on tenors out to 3.5yrs, as there is a material risk that the actual EUR/USD FX rate dips below the FX forward profile. If that happens, and we're long carry (receive SOFR and pay Ester), the value of that carry is wiped out (as we have created a EUR liability that gets hurt on EUR strength). In that case, it's best to be short carry (receive Ester and pay SOFR). That carry cost ranges from -c.160bp in the 1yr out to -c.140bp in the 3yr (see charts below).

For longer tenors, the implied breakeven for long carry plays is above 1.25 (as gleaned from the FX forward profile above). The area of real comfort is in the 7yr tenor, which has an FX forward rate of 1.31 (and the 10yr is even more comfortable at 1.36).

The simple prognosis here is long carry is comfortable in longer tenors, provided there is a reasonable degree of comfort that EUR/USD does not venture above 1.25, with the 1.3 area as the ultimate pain point.

US spreads over eurozone (Ester into SOFR)

Chart on right includes the cross currency basis - practically invisible (as at or around zero)

The basis piece is not a factor, but the FX entry point actually is

The EUR/USD cross-currency basis, which is effectively an arbitrage between actual spreads and FX forwards, is practically zero at the moment. It used to reflect a sizeable dollar premium, which resulted in wider spreads than advertised (often, typically by some 30bp).

That has disappeared and is liable to remain absent. That's good for the negative carry play, as it means no amplification of spreads. While for positive carry plays, it means that a prior positive arbitrage has gone, but at least has not flipped to a space where it actually hurts.

One clear plus for the positive carry play is the FX entry point has improved. Get in at as strong an EUR/USD rate as possible, and the risk for subsequent EUR/USD strength is reduced. Also, the FX forward profile shifts higher too, marking out a better breakeven prognosis.

With respect to absolute spreads, the table below shows that absolute spreads are a tad lower versus their historical averages (by some 10bp). Still good entry points on absolute spreads ranging from 125bp to 150bp. And we expect these spreads to be biased tighter, towards the 100-125bp area, mostly from the Ester side. But not much in it.

Here are the detailed numbers...

Performance Overview

In the quarter, Health Care, Communication Services, and Information Technology contributed the most to absolute returns. Alphabet (GOOGL), which was among the portfolio’s largest positions for much of 2025, continued its strong run in the fourth quarter with accelerating fundamentals augmented by further re-rating in the stock. Danaher (DHR) rallied to close the year as business fundamentals regained their long-term trajectory after a prolonged downturn in the industry. Lam Research (LRCX), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) were also top-five contributors to returns. The largest detractors from absolute quarterly returns were Financials, Industrials, and Real Estate. Fiserv (FISV) declined significantly in October, contributing meaningfully to the fund’s underperformance. Cadence (CDNS), Linde (LIN), United Rentals (URI), and Progressive (PGR) rounded out the top-five detractors in the quarter.

For the full year of 2025, the top-five contributors to returns were Lam Research, Alphabet, Amphenol (APH), Taiwan Semiconductor, and ASML (ASML), and the top-five detractors from returns were Fiserv, Chipotle (CMG), Constellation Software (CNSWF), Roper (ROP), and Floor & Decor (FND). Our strategy’s relative underperformance in the year was more than explained by Fiserv and our underweight in Western Europe.

In a surprise move, Fiserv’s new management reset their operating model and communicated systematic over-earning under prior management, who, it appears, prioritized near-term revenue streams and cost-cutting initiatives rather than long-term, durable growth. In NZS terms, the previous team had been sacrificing duration and longevity for near-term efficiency, which is the opposite of what we look for in investments. Our lesson with Fiserv is one we’ve unfortunately been taught before: a good company trading at a great valuation does not equal a resilient position. While we remain valuation disciplined, the prediction of future earnings can be quite fragile to ranges of outcomes around the core business (to the downside and the upside). An attractive valuation cannot compensate for unhealth in the organization, just as great companies can often make expensive valuations appear cheap in retrospect. While we appreciate the new CEO of Fiserv “coming clean”, with investors, we exited the position as it became clear that we had evaluated the company incorrectly and that it was neither resilient nor optional.

In Western Europe, banks, pharmaceuticals, energy, defense, and tobacco companies had a strong year of performance. We generally do not find investments that fit our framework in those specific industries. The region also experienced a material tailwind to USD returns from a strengthening euro. For the time being, we are comfortable with our positioning in every geography, and we will continue to scour the globe for adaptable businesses that are creating non-zero-sum outcomes in their ecosystems.

Portfolio Positioning

After adding to IT names amid weakness in the first half of 2025, we began reallocating outside of IT in the third quarter as AI euphoria returned, multiples in the sector recovered, and we found incrementally more attractive opportunities in other sectors like software (see below). In the backdrop of a myopically focused market, we are finding a growing opportunity set of left-behind, “quality growth” companies trading at attractive valuations. In the quarter, we added several names to the portfolio that would loosely fit this description – Danaher and Linde to name two – and introduced new positions with Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG), an insurance broker, and Dino Polska (DNOPF), a fast-growing Polish grocer. New optionality positions were also started with JFrog (FROG) and Heartflow (HTFL). In the quarter, we exited Fiserv, Chipotle, and monday.com. The portfolio remains overweight IT, although at a level below where we entered 2025.

Artificially Disrupted?

As recently as 12 months ago, valuations for cloud software companies were among the highest in the market. This reflected the long-time fundamentals of the sector: attractive growth rates, sticky and predictable revenue streams, enviable gross margins with little marginal costs, negative working capital, etc. In 2025, the market created a wall of worry that AI would displace existing software companies based on the assumption that white-collar job losses and disruption from AI agents would obsolete some of the most important companies in the world.

We agree the range of outcomes has widened, but we think there is reason to be more optimistic about the software industry’s ability to adapt to the new age of AI. The first indicator of adaptability we look for is non-zero sumness, or NZS. We think software systems of record (SoR) are generally starting from a position of positive NZS – they are often mission critical, deeply embedded in an organization, and contain the key data and workflow context needed to transform a workflow through AI; however, they generally represent a low-single-digit expense as a percentage of revenue. Vertical market software platforms further leverage domain expertise to deliver purpose-built functionality for their respective niches and to inform which features to prioritize. In essence, customers of B2B software rely heavily on their SoR and generally are not itching to ditch them.

In a customer’s ideal world, they would leverage AI within their existing software. Here, another foundational element of adaptability for cloud software is its ability to be frequently versioned. Potentially driven by paranoia and/or reacting to stock price declines, we have seen software platforms relatively aggressively introduce AI functionality. This augmentation has occurred either through internal development on top of the foundational models or via APIs for third-party tools. Customer adoption of these features has been mixed, but we expect continued progress in 2026 and beyond. The important point is that the existing SoR do not appear to be complacent and are ensuring that their users can productively leverage AI within their existing workflows. So long as this is the case, we think startup competitors will face the same historical challenges in their attempt to displace the incumbents. Intense competition is not new in most categories of software, after all.

In summary, do we think AI has widened the range of outcomes for software? Yes. Do we think the market has taken a rather draconian position on the subject? Probably. We also believe there is a compelling fundamental bull case in the range of outcomes where AI leads to reduced churn, new revenue streams, and accelerated operating margin expansion (software companies are arguably the biggest beneficiaries of AI efficiency gains). In the short term, we expect software’s day-to-day moves to be driven by the markets’ fears of AI and decelerating fundamentals; however, longer-term, we think software represents one of the more interesting opportunities in the market. Our portfolio’s software positioning remains focused primarily on SoR and vertical market software, which we think have the best chance of adapting.

The following market commentary is excerpted from NZS Capital Investor, Brad Slingerlend’s, December Newsletter, SITALWeek #460

We are no strangers to bubbles and narrow markets, having invested through some interesting times during the last several decades. Indeed, our Complexity Investing framework was created to explain how to invest in an unpredictable world. One strategy we use in times like this is to shift narrow predictions to broader predictions, both at the individual stock level and the overall portfolio level. At one point, a few months ago, I expressed to our investors that the market was evolving into a single prediction: one AI startup was going to build a multi-hundred-billion-dollar business in just 2-3 years. Could it happen? Sure, but is it the most likely outcome? Probably not. Are we concerned about circular revenue (aka vendor financing, as we called it during the telecom bubble) and increased leverage? Yes. The fact that everyone seems to be a consenting participants doesn’t make it OK. It reminds me of the great Penn & Teller cup-and-ball trick done with transparent cups. Even in plain sight, it’s still a trick. There is no better metaphor for market bubbles than the transparent cup and balls – despite being in on the trick, it still fools you. Our job as investors today, in terms of how we think about the world at NZS Capital, is to identify and seek to take advantage of the broader (higher probability) predictions that may be connected to the markets’ AI gamble. For example, we can create a more agnostic portfolio in which we carefully match positions to the range of outcomes encompassed by two diametrically opposed world views: in one world, AI meets investors’ high expectations and we are off to the races; in the other, classic negative feedback loops and efficiency gains create a more predictable, linear progress curve for AI’s impact on the world, economy, and markets. My hope is actually for a linear impact, because I think it’s important that the pace of change is slow enough to allow entrepreneurial job creation to replace those lost to AI. But, as they say, hope in one hand and spit in the other, and see which fills up faster.

That tongue-in-cheek aside, I am not, in fact, overly negative about the bumpy road ahead. Indeed, I’ve found it much more productive to view the world through the lens of skeptical optimism. As I’ve noted in the past, neither cynicism nor pessimism have been winning long-term investment strategies. Skepticism is always prudent, but optimism is the only way to make money in the long term. In many ways, it’s nearly impossible to have a successful investment strategy or philosophy that expresses pessimism when the world is optimistic. It does work occasionally, just as a stopped clock is right twice a day. However, by being optimistic when the world is pessimistic, you are more likely to collect the treasure that’s lying around the economic landscape. You don’t even need to find the end of the rainbow. So, now is a good time to prepare to be optimistic for when the world turns pessimistic. It was Buffett who famously said: “be fearful when others are greedy, and be greedy when others are fearful”. I think the first part of Buffett’s aphorism is a tough way to make a living as an investor, given that things are always getting better over time, and the second half sounds a little too cynical to me. So, with a great deal of unmerited hubris, let’s rewrite that quote as: “Be appropriately skeptical when others are optimistic, be optimistic when others are pessimistic, and never be cynical”. OK, I admit, Buffett’s is a tad more catchy. Another bit of traditional advice for long-term investors, often trumpeted by Buffett in the past, is to own a passive index like the S&P 500. I wonder if that suggestion needs some updating as well, given the current, narrow prediction that’s overtaken the stock markets? At the moment, the top five stocks in the S&P 500 are around 30% of the index, and they are all highly correlated with each other (NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon). Is that a diversified long-term portfolio, or is it a risky, concentrated bet on one theme? A more appropriate approach today might be closer to the agnostic – but still very active – portfolio construction mentioned above. The good news for investors today is that the narrow, single-prediction stock market has resulted in some fantastic, high quality growth businesses being left for dead across many sectors (for example, software, where I believe systems of record and vertical market software are well positioned for either AI path the world may take).

This discussion of ephemeral bubbles reminds me of one of my favorite quotes from one of my favorite movies, Harvey (based on the original play). At one point Elwood P. Dowd, played by Jimmy Stewart, is at a bar, perhaps with a 6’2” tall seemingly invisible rabbit, and Mr. Wilson from the sanitarium asks the bartender about Dowd, “Is he alone?” To which the bartender replies, “Well, there’s two schools of thought, sir”. Right now, there are very much two schools of thought. Perhaps that mischievous pooka is real, or perhaps not, but we certainly all want to believe in the big bunny. So, as the evening wears on, we remain skeptically optimistic for the near future as we see how the AI platform wars shake out. Over the longer time horizon, we are giddy for the explosion of new ideas, companies, and scientific revolutions that the massive AI platforms will enable, which I look forward to writing about in the coming years.

Conclusion

We’re deeply grateful for your continued support and trust , which is central to everything we do. The year 2025 marked NZS’s sixth year of operations, and we are greatly encouraged by the engagement we’ve had from existing, new, and prospective investors. We feel extremely fortunate to have grown the business, exiting 2025 with >$4B under management for the first time. We’re energized by the opportunity set for the portfolio and NZS Capital as we enter 2026.

As always, we welcome your questions, comments, thoughts, and ideas.

The NZS Team

data.title: Lyrical Asset Management 2025 U.S. Value Review Letter
data.description:
2025 was a good year for Lyrical with our CS composite generating a 17.9% net return, matching the S&P 500 and significantly outperforming the S&P 500 Value by 470 bps. Keeping pace with the S&P 500 was difficult this year. The index is capitalization-weighted, and its returns were inflated by outsized contributions from a small number of very large growth stocks. Those mega cap stocks skewed the index return so much that 70% of S&P 500 constituents underperformed, and the S&P 500 Equal Weight index returned only 11.4%, 6.5 percentage points less than the S&P 500 and Lyrical.

In large part due to the AI trade, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has now outperformed the equal weight version by a record amount over the last three years. And while it has been most extreme over that period, the cycle of cap weighted outperformance has had a longer history dating back to March 2015. Cumulatively, the outperformance has become substantial. Over nearly 11 years, the S&P 500 has returned 300%, 109 percentage points ahead of the 191% return of the S&P 500 Equal Weight.

Importantly, this excess return of the S&P 500 was not driven by faster earnings growth, but rather due to a soaring valuation multiple. This leaves the S&P 500 index very expensive by historical standards, and at a lofty 32% premium relative to the equal weight index at year end.

Unless the future growth of the cap-weighted index significantly outpaces the equal weight index, we would expect the S&P 500 valuation to fall back in line with the equal weight index and its own history, unwinding the thousands of basis points of outperformance it has generated over the last few years. It is easy to forget that the equal weight index has been the better performer historically, and that to date, every time mega caps have outperformed, they eventually went on to underperform, losing all their outperformance and then some. If that happens, we believe it would be a very favorable environment for our value investing approach.

Even without S&P 500 valuation reversion, we are confident in our ability to perform given the growth and valuation characteristics of our portfolio. Our current portfolio has a history of 10.6% EPS growth, four percentage points faster than the 6.6% growth of the S&P 500. Yet, despite that faster growth, our portfolio also has an attractive valuation of only 12.5x forward earnings at year end, while the slower growing S&P 500 valuation is 78% higher at 22.2x.

If our companies continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, the wide valuation discount should justifiably narrow, delivering outperformance. That is the Lyrical formula for success.

S&P 500 Performance: Capitalization vs. Equal Weight

Historically, the equal weight S&P 500 has outperformed the cap-weighted S&P 500 over time. As we show below in the graph on the left, from the inception of the S&P 500 Equal Weight in 1990 through March 2015, a period of about 25 years, the equal weight index outperformed the S&P 500 by 1.9 percent per annum.

However, as shown in the graph on the right, over the last ~11 years since then, it is the cap weighted index that has outperformed by an average of 3.3 percent per annum, and this has lowered the historical outperformance of the S&P 500 Equal Weight to just 0.3 percent per annum over the full 36-year history.

Source: FactSet

Within the last 11 years of cap weight outperformance, there are clearly two distinct periods, which are also shown above in the graph on the right. For the first 8 years, the cap-weighted index mildly outperformed by 80 basis points per year. But over the last three years the cap-weighted index outperformance has been massive at more than 1,000 basis points per year. In fact, this three-year period is even more extreme than the tech bubble era and the most extreme we have seen in the entire history of the equal weight index.

Is it sustainable?

In total, the 3.3% annualized outperformance of the cap weight index over these last 11 years compounds to 109 percentage points! The question going forward is whether cap weighted outperformance is sustainable. As we analyze the drivers of outperformance over the last 11 years, our conclusion is that it is not.

Earnings for both indices have been almost identical. In the left-hand graph below, we show the growth in forward earnings of the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Equal Weight over the 11-year period that the cap weight index has been outperforming. Both indices grew at essentially the same rate, such that over these 11 years, the cap weight earnings are only 1.6% higher than the Equal Weight earnings, an annualized difference of about 10 basis points per year. Thus, fundamental earnings growth did not explain the huge performance difference.

Nearly all the outperformance was driven by the valuation of the S&P 500 becoming historically expensive. In the right-hand graph below, we show the change in valuation for the two indices. Eleven years ago, the two indices had similar multiples. In fact, the equal weight index multiple was one point higher at 17.3x compared to 16.2x for the cap-weighted index. At the end of 2025, the multiple comparison was quite different. Despite essentially the same historical growth over the past 11 years, the cap-weighted index multiple is now 32% higher. That is a massive premium to pay for something that has not historically offered materially better growth.

Source: FactSet

Compared to the equal weight index, and its own history, the S&P 500 multiple simply looks too high. Unless the future growth of the cap-weighted index significantly outpaces the equal weight index, we would expect the valuation multiple to fall back in line with the equal weight index and its own history, unwinding the thousands of basis points of outperformance it has generated over the last few years.

How did we keep up?

At Lyrical, we have not owned any of the mega cap growth stocks that have disproportionately driven the S&P 500 returns, yet we still were able to match the S&P 500 performance and significantly outperform the S&P 500 Equal Weight and S&P 500 Value indices.

While we have not owned any of the expensive AI-related mega-cap growth stocks, our top four contributors in 2025 were attractively valued companies that benefitted from the expected spending on AI technology. These stocks, shown in the table below, included NRG Energy (an independent power producer), Johnson Controls (a provider of HVAC products and services), FLEX (a contract manufacturer and leading provider of critical equipment to hyperscalers), and United Rentals (the world’s largest construction equipment rental company).

Source: FactSet

These stocks entered 2025 at an average discount to the S&P 500 of over 30% and some are still undervalued heading into 2026. Despite its 79% net return, NRG Energy (NRG) is still attractively valued at just 13.7x forward earnings when we normalize depreciation expense and add the accretion from the company’s LS Power acquisition. Flex (FLEX) is also still attractive after a net return of 57%. Flex finished the year with a forward P/E of 17.5x, a 21% discount to the S&P 500 despite its much higher growth.

Closer to fair value is Johnson Controls (JCI), which ended the year with a forward P/E of 22.8x adjusting for non-cash amortization. Lastly, United Rentals (URI) reached our estimate of fair value in September after a 36% increase year-to-date, and we sold it from the portfolio.

Not all earnings growth was rewarded

While our AI-related value stocks provided a boost to 2025 returns, severe multiple compression in a large portion of the portfolio kept us from having an even better year.

Throughout the year, fundamentals were strong across our portfolio. Apart from Centene (CNC), and to a much lesser degree Arrow and LKQ (LKQ), every other portfolio company increased EPS in 2025. As we would expect, most of our stocks with good fundamentals had good stock performance. As we show in the top row of the table below, 15 of our current companies, accounting for almost 60% of the portfolio weight, experienced an average of 22% growth in forward earnings, compounded by 18% multiple expansion, leading to an average return of 42%. These winners were still attractively valued at year-end with an average P/E of just 13.6x, a 39% discount to the 22.2x P/E of the S&P 500.

Excludes: CNC, ICLR (ICLR) & LKQSource: Lyrical analysis

Those stocks propelled our returns and are the reason why we had a good 2025. But the year could have been even better if we did not suffer from multiple compression in most of the remaining portfolio. As we show in the bottom row of the table, we had 13 other stocks accounting for 34% of portfolio weight that increased forward earnings by 10% on average in 2025. Yet, despite increasing earnings, those stocks declined due to an average of 17% multiple compression. These companies ended the year at a valuation of just 10.5x, a 53% discount to the S&P 500. If those stocks had just maintained their cheap multiples instead of getting cheaper, that would have contributed over 500 basis points to our returns. Given their fundamentals and now even lower valuation, we believe they could be a major contributor to performance in 2026 and beyond.

Our valuation spread remains wide

We continue to believe our portfolio is well positioned. In the graph below, we show our U.S. portfolio valuation relative to our historical portfolio P/E ratios, represented by the solid and dotted dark blue lines.

Source: FactSet

Our CS composite P/E stands at 12.5x. The S&P 500 P/E, which is represented by the bright blue line, is up to 22.2x, and stands 36% above its average since our inception, 32% above the S&P 500 Equal Weight, and 83% higher than our current EQ portfolio. That is significantly above the spread we observed during the first decade of our firm, when it averaged ~30%.

Our uncommon combination

Not only is our portfolio significantly cheaper than the S&P 500 (shown again below in the bar chart on the left), but our portfolio also has a faster growth profile, as shown in the line graph on the right.

The lines at the top show the EPS growth history of our current CS and EQ composites, while the lines below them show the EPS growth of the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Value.

Dating back to 2007, the S&P 500 has had an annualized EPS growth history of 6.6%. Our current portfolio has a much better history of 10.6% EPS growth, four percentage points faster than the S&P 500.

Our confidence in our ability to outperform is rooted in these two statistics of valuation and growth. If our portfolio’s earnings continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, we should be able to perform as well or slightly better, as long as our relative multiple stays where it is. But if our companies continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, the wide discount should justifiably narrow, delivering additional outperformance.

Source: FactSet. See notes below

Conclusion

2025 was a good year for Lyrical in which we produced strong absolute returns of 17.9% matching the S&P 500 despite not owning Magnificent Seven stocks. Moreover, we outperformed both the S&P 500 Value and S&P 500 Equal Weight by 4.7 and 6.5 percentage points, respectively.

Looking forward, the biggest issue we see in the market is the high valuation of the S&P 500. The S&P 500 has outperformed the equal weight index by over 10 percentage points per year for the last three years, which is a record amount. As a result, the S&P 500 looks very expensive, both relative to its own history, and also relative to the S&P 500 equal weight.

If the S&P 500 valuation reverts back to its historical average and in line with the S&P 500 equal weight, we should benefit as we do not own the mega-cap growth stocks that have inflated the index returns and valuation. However, what ultimately should drive performance of Lyrical portfolios is our uncommon combination of both discounted valuation and faster earnings growth. The value spread between our portfolio and the S&P 500 is historically wide at 78%. This compares to about a 30% spread during the first decade of our firm. This spread persists despite the superior growth history of our stocks. Our companies have an average historical EPS CAGR of over 10.6%, four percentage points faster than the S&P 500 growth of 6.6%.

Fundamentals have not always mattered to the market over the last five years. This was evident again in 2025 when 34% of our portfolio had increasing earnings but decreasing multiples. While fundamentals can be ignored in the short term, we maintain our core belief that earnings fundamentals are all that matter in the long term. If our companies keep compounding earnings, their stock prices should follow. Additionally, if their growth continues to match or outpace the S&P 500, their valuations should expand too, increasing their stock prices even more.

That is the Lyrical way.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the strategy will be achieved. Risks of an investment in this strategy include, but are not limited to, the risks of investing in equity securities generally, and in a value investing approach. Where we provide information about performance that is not the actual performance results of our investment strategies (such as where we show the hypothetical results under the caption “Simple Math,” above), please note that there are substantial additional limitations inherent in using such performance information. Those include, but are not limited to, that actual trading and the associated expenses did not occur, that market conditions change over time, and that no investor had the actual performance presented.

Past performance is not necessarily indicative of future results.

LAM - EQ results are unaudited and subject to revision, are for a composite of all accounts, and show all periods beginning with the first full month in which the advisor managed its first fee-paying account. The S&P 500® Equal Weight Index is the equal-weight version of the widely-used S&P 500. The index includes the same constituents as the capitalization weighted S&P 500, but each company in the S&P 500 EWI is allocated a fixed weight – or 0.2% of the index total at each quarterly rebalance.
